refined_fr.refined_fr: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a numerical availability target, and (2) doing so within a defined measurement window. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent design decisions and verification strategies. The refined FR focuses on the primary functional obligation—sustaining availability performance—while treating the measurement period as a constraint context rather than a functional requirement. This formulation is solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, predictive maintenance, or any specific DP; it only states the availability outcome required.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total time − unplanned downtime) / total time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold at the end of the evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: Establish and communicate the availability measurement methodology and evaluation period to all stakeholders.
FR-NGHE-004.3: Provide visibility into availability performance (uptime/downtime events) to Site Manager and Project Manager during the evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-004.1a: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity during component degradation or failure events.

DP-NGHE-004.1b: Design preventive maintenance scheduling and condition-monitoring protocols that detect and replace wear-prone components before failure thresholds are reached, reducing unplanned downtime.

DP-NGHE-004.1c: Establish rapid fault detection and isolation mechanisms (diagnostics, self-test routines) coupled with standardized repair procedures and spare-parts availability to minimize mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR).
DP-NGHE-004.1d: Architect modular, hot-swappable subsystem design with standardized interfaces to enable in-field replacement without full system shutdown, reducing downtime per failure event.

DP-NGHE-004.1e: Implement graceful degradation logic that allows the system to operate at reduced capacity (e.g., lower throughput, limited functionality) rather than total failure, preserving partial availability during component faults.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-004.1: Achieve target availability through a composite strategy combining (1) redundancy and failover for critical subsystems, (2) predictive maintenance and condition monitoring to prevent failures, (3) rapid fault detection and repair procedures to minimize MTTR, and (4) modular hot-swappable architecture to enable in-field recovery without full shutdown. Graceful degradation logic preserves partial availability during transient faults.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP-004.1a) is tightly coupled to control logic and failover timing; must be decoupled from specific subsystem implementations to avoid over-specification.
Maintenance scheduling (DP-004.1b) depends on accurate failure-rate data and operational context; coupling risk if maintenance intervals are not independently tunable per deployment environment.

Fault detection (DP-004.1c) and modular architecture (DP-004.1d) are moderately coupled; isolation mechanisms must be designed to work across multiple module boundaries without creating hidden dependencies.
Graceful degradation (DP-004.1e) introduces coupling between nominal and reduced-capacity operating modes; must ensure mode transitions do not cascade failures or violate safety constraints.
coupling_check.notes: The composite DP is logically sound but requires careful decomposition into independent sub-DPs at the next level to avoid architectural coupling. Redundancy and modularity are complementary; failover logic must be abstracted from subsystem details. Maintenance and diagnostics should be decoupled from operational control to allow independent tuning.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and weight/space budgets; risk: redundancy overhead may exceed acceptable limits for resource-constrained deployments.
Assumption: Failure modes are well-characterized and predictable; risk: unknown failure modes or cascading failures may not be mitigated by the selected DP.
Assumption: Spare parts and repair expertise are available on-site or accessible within acceptable lead times; risk: supply-chain delays or lack of trained personnel could violate MTTR targets.

Assumption: Graceful degradation does not violate functional or safety requirements; risk: reduced-capacity operation may be unsafe or unacceptable for certain worksite conditions (e.g., load-bearing, safety-critical tasks).

Assumption: Modular hot-swap design is feasible without introducing new failure modes (e.g., connector wear, misalignment); risk: modularity may increase complexity and introduce new points of failure.

Assumption: Condition-monitoring sensors and diagnostics are reliable and do not themselves become availability bottlenecks; risk: monitoring infrastructure failure could mask or delay detection of primary faults.

Assumption: Evaluation period is representative of steady-state operation; risk: startup transients, seasonal variations, or worksite-specific stressors may not be captured in the agreed evaluation window.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-004.1 is well-formed and the selected composite DP is appropriate. Recommend decomposing the DP into independent sub-DPs at the next hierarchical level: (1) DP-004.1.1 for redundancy and failover architecture, (2) DP-004.1.2 for maintenance and condition monitoring, (3) DP-004.1.3 for fault detection and repair procedures, (4) DP-004.1.4 for modular hot-swap design, and (5) DP-004.1.5 for graceful degradation logic. Each sub-DP should be mapped to distinct implementation modules (IM) to enable independent ownership, testing, and evolution. Additionally, clarify acceptance criteria: specify whether 0.97 availability is measured as uptime ratio, mean-time-between-failures (MTBF), or a composite metric; define evaluation period length and operational profile (duty cycle, environmental conditions, worksite constraints) to ensure measurability and reproducibility.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance orchestration, fault detection routing, and graceful degradation logic across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services)

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health metrics, sensor data, and operational state from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive maintenance and condition monitoring

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Directs failover commands, maintenance scheduling, and graceful degradation mode transitions to Vehicle and Teleoperation

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives critical fault notifications from Safety Services; broadcasts availability state changes and degradation alerts back to Safety Services

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Coordinates redundancy failover signaling and rapid fault detection across distributed subsystem instances

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestrator responsible for composite availability strategy. Redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance scheduling, fault detection routing, and graceful degradation logic all naturally reside at this level. Minor diffusion: actual failover execution and hot-swap mechanics partially diffuse into Vehicle module, but control logic and decision-making remain centralized.

Weak containment: Vehicle is a subsystem, not a system-level orchestrator. While it implements modular hot-swappable architecture and local fault detection, the composite strategy (redundancy across subsystems, predictive maintenance scheduling, graceful degradation across Teleoperation and Safety Services) cannot be owned by Vehicle alone. High diffusion: coordination logic would leak into Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote operation. It cannot own system-level availability strategy that spans Vehicle, Safety Services, and its own redundancy. Predictive maintenance and cross-subsystem failover logic would diffuse significantly.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a specialized subsystem for safety-critical functions. While it participates in availability (e.g., receiving alerts, enforcing safe degradation), it cannot own the composite availability strategy. Ownership would create circular dependencies and diffuse core logic across Vehicle and Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-NGHE-004.1 is a system-level availability architecture that requires orchestration across all subsystems. NGHE Solution, as the top-level system boundary, is the natural owner. It coordinates redundancy, predictive maintenance, fault detection, and graceful degradation across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Subsystems implement local mechanisms (hot-swap, local monitoring) but do not own the composite strategy.
containment_risks: Risk: Failover execution and hot-swap mechanics in Vehicle may require tight coupling to NGHE Solution's availability decisions, creating potential latency in fault response.
Risk: Predictive maintenance scheduling may require deep knowledge of subsystem-specific degradation patterns; centralized logic could become complex if subsystems have heterogeneous health models.

Risk: Graceful degradation logic must coordinate across Safety Services constraints; if Safety Services imposes hard constraints that conflict with availability goals, resolution logic may become convoluted.

Risk: Network Link reliability is assumed; if Network Link itself fails, distributed redundancy coordination becomes unreliable. Mitigation requires local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP-NGHE-004.1 is well-formulated and appropriately scoped at the system level. No revision needed. However, to reduce diffusion risk, consider decomposing into sub-DPs: (1) DP-NGHE-004.1a: Redundancy and failover orchestration (NGHE Solution owns decision logic; Vehicle/Teleoperation own execution), (2) DP-NGHE-004.1b: Predictive maintenance and condition monitoring (NGHE Solution owns scheduling; subsystems own local sensors), (3) DP-NGHE-004.1c: Rapid fault detection and repair procedures (NGHE Solution owns routing; Safety Services owns validation), (4) DP-NGHE-004.1d: Modular hot-swappable architecture (Vehicle owns design; NGHE Solution owns orchestration). This preserves the composite strategy while clarifying ownership boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability target (≥0.97) is measurable and traceable to NGHE Solution as system-level orchestrator. Telemetry ingestion, Tasking directives, SafetyAlert coordination, and Network Link signaling provide observable interfaces for validation. Acceptance criteria can be verified through operational metrics (MTTR, failover response time, graceful degradation activation logs) collected at NGHE Solution boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004.1 is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level availability architecture owner. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) have well-defined supporting roles: Vehicle implements hot-swappable mechanics and local fault detection; Teleoperation provides redundant command channels; Safety Services enforces safe degradation constraints. Ownership boundary is unambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-004.1 directly addresses FR-NGHE-004.1 through a composite strategy with four complementary mechanisms: (1) redundancy and failover reduce single-point-of-failure impact, (2) predictive maintenance prevents failures proactively, (3) rapid fault detection minimizes MTTR, (4) modular hot-swappable architecture enables in-field recovery. Graceful degradation preserves partial availability during transients. Strategy is logically sound and sufficient to achieve ≥0.97 availability target when properly implemented. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Failover execution and hot-swap mechanics in Vehicle subsystem are tightly coupled to NGHE Solution's availability decisions; latency in Network Link or decision routing could delay fault response and violate MTTR targets. Mitigation: define maximum decision-to-execution latency SLA and implement local failover autonomy in Vehicle for network partition scenarios.

Predictive maintenance scheduling logic may require deep subsystem-specific health models (e.g., motor degradation patterns, teleoperation link quality trends); centralizing this in NGHE Solution risks complexity growth and maintenance burden. Mitigation: decompose into DP-NGHE-004.1b with subsystems owning local health model definitions and NGHE Solution owning cross-subsystem scheduling orchestration.

Graceful degradation logic must coordinate with Safety Services hard constraints (e.g., minimum safety monitoring availability); conflicting requirements could create convoluted resolution logic at NGHE Solution boundary. Mitigation: establish explicit safety-availability trade-off matrix and encode as decision rules in NGHE Solution; validate against worksite safety plan.

Network Link reliability is assumed for distributed redundancy coordination; if Network Link fails, NGHE Solution loses visibility and control authority. Mitigation: implement local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation; define fallback modes that do not require NGHE Solution coordination.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is acceptable for a system-level orchestrator. The minor diffusion into Vehicle (failover execution, hot-swap mechanics) is appropriate: NGHE Solution owns decision logic and coordination; Vehicle owns physical implementation. Subsystems (Vehicle: 0.45, Teleoperation: 0.35, Safety Services: 0.30) correctly show low containment scores, confirming they are not suitable owners of the composite strategy. Ownership allocation is sound. To further reduce diffusion risk and improve clarity, recommend decomposing DP-NGHE-004.1 into four sub-DPs with explicit ownership boundaries as outlined in provided recommendation.

recommended_revisions: Decompose DP-NGHE-004.1 into four sub-DPs to clarify ownership and reduce diffusion: (1) DP-NGHE-004.1a: Redundancy and failover orchestration (NGHE Solution owns decision logic; Vehicle/Teleoperation own execution), (2) DP-NGHE-004.1b: Predictive maintenance and condition monitoring (NGHE Solution owns scheduling; subsystems own local health sensors and models), (3) DP-NGHE-004.1c: Rapid fault detection and repair procedures (NGHE Solution owns routing and escalation; Safety Services owns validation and safe shutdown), (4) DP-NGHE-004.1d: Modular hot-swappable architecture (Vehicle owns mechanical/electrical design; NGHE Solution owns orchestration of swap procedures).

Define maximum decision-to-execution latency SLA for failover commands (e.g., ≤100ms) to ensure MTTR targets are achievable. Specify Network Link latency and reliability assumptions explicitly in DP-NGHE-004.1a.

Establish explicit safety-availability trade-off matrix in coordination with Safety Services. Document which graceful degradation modes are permissible under worksite safety plan constraints. Encode as decision rules in NGHE Solution.

Implement local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to handle Network Link partition scenarios. Define fallback modes that do not require NGHE Solution coordination; validate that fallback modes preserve minimum availability target.

Specify health model interface between subsystems and NGHE Solution for predictive maintenance (DP-NGHE-004.1b). Each subsystem must expose standardized health metrics (e.g., degradation rate, remaining useful life) to enable centralized scheduling without requiring NGHE Solution to embed subsystem-specific knowledge.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-NGHE-004.1 and DP-NGHE-004.1 are logically sound and properly allocated to NGHE Solution. However, DP-NGHE-004.1 is a composite strategy spanning four distinct mechanisms (redundancy/failover, predictive maintenance, rapid fault detection, graceful degradation) with non-trivial diffusion risks (0.08) and implementation coupling concerns. The recommended_revisions explicitly call for decomposition into four sub-DPs with clarified ownership boundaries. Given high safety criticality and the complexity of coordinating failover latency, health model interfaces, and safety-availability trade-offs, proceeding directly to implementation without sub-level functional decomposition would create unacceptable architectural ambiguity and integration risk. Decomposing into sub-FRs now enables explicit ownership allocation, latency SLA definition, and safety constraint encoding at the appropriate level of detail before detailed design begins.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1a: Orchestrate redundancy and failover decisions to minimize single-point-of-failure impact and ensure failover execution latency ≤100ms to meet MTTR targets.
FR-NGHE-004.1b: Schedule and coordinate predictive maintenance activities across subsystems based on standardized health metrics to prevent failures proactively.
FR-NGHE-004.1c: Detect faults rapidly and route repair procedures through Safety Services validation to ensure safe degradation and minimize downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.1d: Manage modular hot-swappable component replacement procedures to enable in-field recovery without system shutdown.

revise.why: Before decomposing sub-FRs, revise DP-NGHE-004.1 to explicitly state the four sub-strategies and their ownership boundaries: (1) DP-NGHE-004.1a: NGHE Solution owns failover decision logic and latency SLA enforcement; Vehicle/Teleoperation own execution. (2) DP-NGHE-004.1b: NGHE Solution owns scheduling orchestration; subsystems own local health sensor integration and model definitions via standardized interface. (3) DP-NGHE-004.1c: NGHE Solution owns fault routing and escalation; Safety Services owns validation and safe shutdown enforcement. (4) DP-NGHE-004.1d: Vehicle owns mechanical/electrical hot-swap design; NGHE Solution owns orchestration of swap procedures. This revision clarifies the composite structure and reduces ambiguity before sub-level decomposition.

assumptions: Network Link latency is ≤100ms under normal operation; local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation can handle Network Link partition scenarios without NGHE Solution coordination.
Subsystems can expose standardized health metrics (degradation rate, remaining useful life) via a common interface; NGHE Solution does not require subsystem-specific health model knowledge.
Safety Services can validate graceful degradation modes against worksite safety plan constraints and provide decision rules to NGHE Solution.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems have sufficient local autonomy and sensor coverage to execute failover and hot-swap procedures with ≤100ms latency.
Predictive maintenance scheduling does not require real-time optimization; batch scheduling with subsystem-specific lead times is acceptable.
open_risks: Failover decision-to-execution latency may exceed 100ms if Network Link experiences congestion or if NGHE Solution decision logic is computationally expensive; this could violate MTTR targets and reduce effective availability below 0.97.

Subsystems may not have sufficient local autonomy to execute failover without NGHE Solution coordination; if local autonomy is limited, Network Link partition scenarios could cause cascading failures.

Safety-availability trade-off matrix may reveal conflicting constraints (e.g., Safety Services requires minimum monitoring availability that conflicts with graceful degradation modes); resolution could require system-level redesign.

Health model interface standardization may be infeasible if subsystems have heterogeneous health monitoring architectures; this could force NGHE Solution to embed subsystem-specific knowledge, increasing complexity and maintenance burden.
Hot-swappable component replacement procedures may require manual intervention or specialized tools; if procedures are not fully automated, in-field recovery time could exceed MTTR targets.
evidence_needed: Latency measurements from Network Link under nominal and degraded conditions; confirm ≤100ms decision-to-execution latency is achievable.
Local autonomy assessment for Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; identify which failover and hot-swap procedures can execute without NGHE Solution coordination.
Safety-availability trade-off matrix from Safety Services; document permissible graceful degradation modes and hard constraints.
Health model interface specification from subsystems; confirm standardized metrics can be exposed without requiring NGHE Solution to embed subsystem-specific knowledge.
Hot-swap procedure automation assessment; measure in-field replacement time and confirm it is compatible with MTTR targets.
Operational telemetry from pilot deployments or simulation; validate that composite strategy achieves ≥0.97 availability under representative failure scenarios.
